speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. I see you've been started on Atorvastatin 40mg taken once daily at night since December 13th to help manage your cardiovascular risk.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood pressure reading today was 109 over 61 — the systolic value is well within normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose came back at 14.1 mmol/L, which is significantly elevated and consistent with uncontrolled diabetes.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your labs, symptoms, and clinical assessment, your primary diagnosis is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, confirmed as of December 13th.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been feeling really off lately — not sure if it’s related, but I’m exhausted all the time.

speaker: doctor
text: Fatigue is a very common symptom in uncontrolled diabetes, especially when blood sugar stays high. Let’s dig deeper into that.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also had some dizziness when standing up quickly — could that be connected?

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure was 61 mmHg earlier — on the lower side, which may contribute to those lightheaded episodes.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, fatigue has been constant for about two weeks — worse in the mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate was elevated at 121 beats per minute just now — that’s tachycardic and likely reflects your body’s stress response to hyperglycemia.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been drinking more water and urinating frequently too — my vision’s been blurry sometimes.

speaker: doctor
text: Those are classic signs of hyperglycemia. Let’s check your glucose again before we finalize the plan.

speaker: doctor
text: This second reading shows 10.1 mmol/L — still high, but notably lower than the 14.1 we saw earlier this morning.

speaker: patient
text: I skipped breakfast today because I felt nauseous — maybe that affected it?

speaker: doctor
text: That could explain part of the drop, but we need sustained control. Let’s reassess your vital trends.

speaker: doctor
text: Your repeat heart rate is now 106 bpm — improved from 121, but still elevated.

speaker: doctor
text: And your diastolic pressure has risen to 74 mmHg — a bit more stable than the earlier 61.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your persistent hyperglycemia, symptoms, and suboptimal response to current management, I am going to switch you to Insulin glargine 16 units once daily at night, starting tonight.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — will that help with the fatigue and dizziness?

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked my glucose at home just before coming in, it was 14.1 mmol/L — same as that first reading — and my pulse felt really fast, like 121 again.
